coredata.dc:title: Scalable production, cell toxicity assessment, and plant growth promotion activities of carbon quantum dots derived from low-quality coal feedstock
coredata.dc:creator: Saikia, Monikankana
Singh, Anupama
Dihingia, Anjum
Khare, Puja
Kalita, Jatin
Saikia, Binoy K.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have several novel opto-chemical characteristics and potential uses in biomedicine, imaging, catalysis, and sensing. However, there are cost and technical limitations with scaling up the synthesis of CQDs for fulfilling its current demand. We propose a simplistic, innovative, sustainable, and economically scalable method for the formation of fluorescent nitrogen and sulphur co-doped CQDs (NS-CQDs) by utilizing bulk, low-grade subbituminous coal as feedstock. The co-doped NS-CQDs were identified using a suite of microscopic and spectroscopic approaches. We suggest that heteroatoms (i.e., N and S) are effectively self-doped on CQDs surfaces/edges of the carbon nanostructure either by functionalization or by getting incorporated on the edges during the oxidative process. The coal-derived CQDs show blue and cyan fluorescence with quantum yields of 9.35 and 16.96%, respectively. The cytotoxicity, genotoxicity, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) of the coal-derived CQDs were evaluated using a rodent hepatocyte cell line, which revealed that these CQDs are biocompatible, non-cytotoxic, and non-genotoxic. Moreover, these coal-derived CQDs also have proved to be good carriers of nutrients and microbes for plant growth promotion, effectively behaving as a nanofertilizer. Their phytotoxicity and impact on plant metabolites and antioxidant properties of the carbon quantum dot products have also been examined in our study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Changes in Quantum Yields (QYs) of the optimization study regarding the fabrication of coal-derived CQDs.
Plausible formation mechanism of N and S-doped CQDs from subbituminous coal by oxidative ultrasonication.

(a) TEM image of CC-CQDs (SAED patterns is in insert indicating the presence of (002) and (101) planes of graphitized carbon), (b) corresponding HRTEM image, (c) particle size distribution indicating 4–8 nm sizes of coal-derived CQDs. (d) EDS study indicating the presence of C,O,N and S. (e) TEM images of JC-CQDs (SAED patterns is in insert indicating the presence of (100), (101), and (004) planes of graphitized carbon), (f) corresponding HRTEM image. (g) Particle size distribution indicating 3.8–6-nm sizes of coal-derived CQDs. (h) EDS study indicating the presence of C,O,N and S.
(a) XRD patterns of coal-derived CQDs that is CC-CQDs and JC-CQDs representing the (002) graphitic carbon planes. (b) FTIR spectra for CC-CQDs and JC-CQDs.
Deconvoluted spectra of CC-CQDs for (a) C1s (b) O1s (c) N1s and (d) S2p. XPS deconvoluted spectra of JC-CQDs for (e) C1s (f) O1s (g) N1s and (h) S2p.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectra of synthesized coal-derived CQDs. (b) solubility nature of NS-CQDs in water under natural light and under UV light (365 nm), and Their excitation dependent FL spectra of (c) CC-CQDs showing maximum emission around 440 nm and (d) JC-CQDs showing maximum emission around 450 nm. (e) Time-resolved single-photon counting spectroscopy of the coal-derived CQDs at neutral pH. (f) Zeta potential of coal-derived CQDs showing the negative values signifying it to be highly stable.

(a) Cytotoxicity analysis of the synthesized coal-derived CQDs samples shows maximum cell viability of 90 %. (b) ROX analysis of the synthesized coal-derived CQDs reveals that it did not cause any significant change in the generation of intracellular ROS levels.
DNA fragmentation or Genotoxicity analysis of the coal-derived CQDs showing that the treatments of CQDs at a dose of 200 μg/mL for 24 hrs did not cause DNA fragmentation.
Plant growth activity study for different treatments to examine the impact of coal-derived CQDs on plant metabolites.

Effect of coal-derived CQDs on (a) Biomass yield, (b) Plant length, (c) Length of leaves, (d) Chlorophyll, and (e) Protein; plant metabolites (f) Stevioside and (g) Rebaudioside A; and antioxidants (h) Superoxide dismutase, (i) Catalase, and (j) Proline.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have several novel opto-chemical characteristics and potential uses in biomedicine, imaging, catalysis, and sensing. However, there are cost and technical limitations with scaling up the synthesis of CQDs for fulfilling its current demand. We propose a simplistic, innovative, sustainable, and economically scalable method for the formation of fluorescent nitrogen and sulphur co-doped CQDs (NS-CQDs) by utilizing bulk, low-grade subbituminous coal as feedstock. The co-doped NS-CQDs were identified using a suite of microscopic and spectroscopic approaches. We suggest that heteroatoms (i.e., N and S) are effectively self-doped on CQDs surfaces/edges of the carbon nanostructure either by functionalization or by getting incorporated on the edges during the oxidative process. The coal-derived CQDs show blue and cyan fluorescence with quantum yields of 9.35 and 16.96%, respectively. The cytotoxicity, genotoxicity, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) of the coal-derived CQDs were evaluated using a rodent hepatocyte cell line, which revealed that these CQDs are biocompatible, non-cytotoxic, and non-genotoxic. Moreover, these coal-derived CQDs also have proved to be good carriers of nutrients and microbes for plant growth promotion, effectively behaving as a nanofertilizer. Their phytotoxicity and impact on plant metabolites and antioxidant properties of the carbon quantum dot products have also been examined in our study.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs or CDs) are a unique group of discrete spherical carbon based nanomaterials with dimensions below 10 nm that have drawn much interest owing to their unique opto-physicochemical characteristics. First discovered by Xu et al. [1] through the distillation of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), CQDs comprise a group of core–shell composites consisting of a carbon centre with surface functionalised groups. These include hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine groups, among other chemical groups that make them hydrophilic [2]. These zero-dimensional CQDs are spherical and contain sp2/sp3 carbons with amorphous to monocrystalline centres and are comprised of distinct quantized energy levels [3]. They are environmentally sustainable, have excellent biocompatibility and photo-stability in contrast to conventional semiconductor quantum dots such as CdSe, CdS, and PbS [4]. Other promising physicochemical characteristics include their high chemical and suspension stability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high mechanical/ thermal properties, all of which have useful applications in bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, photocatalysis, photovoltaic devices, and optoelectronics [2,5].

Currently major methods to synthesize fluorescent CQDs include arc-discharge, laser ablation, hydrothermal treatment, microwave treatment, and chemical (strong acid/alkali) oxidation [4–6]. However, these techniques are usually costly, time-consuming, and entail the use of potentially harmful chemicals and often result in the production of limited quantities of CQDs as reported elsewhere [6–9]. Wang et al. [6] reported on the production of graphene quantum dots with a total yield of 1.26 g from 2 g of pyrene by refluxing with HNO3 followed by hydrothermal process. Zhai et al. [7] fabricated fluorescent carbon nanodots through microwave-assisted pyrolysis of 1 g citric acid in the influence of different amine moieties to produce 0.98 g as the final yield. Moreover, S and N- codoped C-dots were synthesized by carbonization and etching of 0.50 g hair fibre at different temperatures (40, 100, and 140 °C) had a yield of 9, 16, and 29 % as was reported by Sun et al. [9] CQDs were fabricated by utilizing carbon resources for instance food waste, black carbon, graphite, anthracite coal, and activated carbon (top-down approach) [10–12]. They have also been prepared from molecular resources like citric acid, glucose, sucrose, and saccharides (bottom-down approach) [7,8]. Mehta and co-workers [13] reported on the advancement in surface and band gap modification of CQDs in order to enhance the activity of nanomaterials with a focus on CQDs for photodegradation of organic pollutants, sensing of heavy metal ions in water and treatment of water and splitting purposes. Moreover several studies emphasised on the fabrication of carbon-based nanostructures such as GQDs, graphitic C3N4 and CQDs composites, employing their enhanced properties for wastewater treatment and improved H2 gas production through water splitting [14,15]. Srivastava et al. [16] reported on the fabrication of biofuel from organic matter and waste sources and also prepared Microbial electrolysis cell that enhanced the yield of H2 gas without the use of nanostructures. Besides, several researchers reported the fabrication of biofuels from organic matter and waste sources with/without emphasis on the role of nanotechnology [17,18,19,20]. Although many studies have reported various methods for the synthesis, characterization, and applications of CQDs, there are still knowledge gaps with respect to the choice of raw materials, improving cost-effectiveness, and scaling up synthesis. Large scale synthesis of CQDs necessitates large volumes of strong chemicals, e.g., acids that raise environmental concerns. Natural carbon sources including biomass and fossil resources are viable and cost-effective alternatives to chemical sources and can be used for the mass-scale generation of CQDs [11,21,22].

Coal is one of the most abundant and accessible sources relative to other CQD precursors such as hydrocarbons. Coal is a heterogeneous mixture with cross-connected polycyclic aromatic and hydroaromatic frameworks linked with aliphatic and ether bindings [23]. It has also been previously focused on the fabrication of CQDs [24–26]. Synthesizing CQDs oxidatively from dissimilar forms/grades (e.g., anthracite and bituminous) of coal is desirable since carbon in the coal structure is amenable to modification over pure sp2-carbon frameworks [27]. Ye et al. [23] discussed the use of bituminous coal for synthesising graphene quantum dots by using sulphuric and nitric acids which are also apparently not scalable due to the environmental stringent of acids used. Various parameters such as precursor concentration ratio, solvent ratio, reaction time and temperature, and pH have been reported to affect the particle size and fluorescence intensity of CQDs [25,28,29]. However, only few studies to date have investigated the optimization of the synthesis method of CQDs from non-coal sources [30–35]. Tadesse and his group fabricated luminescent N-doped CQDs from citrus lemon via hydrothermal technique and studied to optimize the procedure in which the optimum reaction condition was 200 °C for 3 h carbonization [30]. In another work, Monte-Filho and co-researchers analysed to optimize the reactions parameters regarding the fabrication of CDs from a mixer of lemon and onion juice via microwave irradiation, in which they found that 6 min irradiation along with 20:2 lemon juice to onion juice ratio yields maximum quantum yield [31]. But, Pudza et al.[33] studied response surface methodology (RSM) to obtain the optimum reaction parameters to increase yield of highly fluorescent carbon dots. Building on these studies, we have devised a method for fabrication of fluorescent CQDs derived from subbituminous coal (low-grade coal consisting of 71–77 wt% carbon in general) and scaling up the process to increase yields.

Thus, for the first time, we have reported the utilization of low-grade subbituminous coals as feedstock for the aqueous synthesis of blue-fluorescent CQDs through low-energy ultrasonic irradiation at an ambient condition. Such abundant low-grade high-sulfur subbituminous coal, unsuitable for steam coal applications, has not been studied by any other researchers for production of high-value CQDs except other coal geochemical studies. This type of coal is considerably rich in carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and sulphur, alongside a small proportion of nitrogen; both nitrogen and sulphur are favourable for the synthesis of self-/co-doped N and S carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) in this simple chemical process. Biocompatibility and bioavailability are the main factors that should be considered while utilizing the CQDs for bioimaging applications [36,37]. We use a suite of analytical techniques to characterize the coal-derived CQDs and determine their toxicity using various biological assays.

The low-grade subbituminous Cenozoic coal samples i.e., Changki coal (referred to as Coal-CC) and Jadi coal (referred to as Coal-JC) were obtained from the North-eastern coalfield of India. The samples were processed according to ASTM [38] standard methods. Briefly, 1 kg of the raw coal samples were crushed and sieved to sizes of about 0.2 mm for subsequent experiments. Analytical grade hydrogen peroxide (30%, Merck) was used to exfoliate coal and ammonium hydroxide solution (25%) was used for neutralization purpose. Quinine sulphate and 0.1 M H2SO4 solution was used to determine the quantum yield of the fabricated CQDs.

Coal-derived CQDs were produced through a modified wet-chemical ultrasonic irradiated technique by mixing 250 g of either coal sample with 2500 mL of H2O2 (30%) in an ice-cold bath. The oxidized material was subjected to ultrasonication (40 kHz) for 6 h at normal pressure and temperature in an ultrasonicator (Powersonic 520). The resulting oxidized mixture was subsequently treated with a solution of ammonium hydroxide (25%) to reach pH 7. The neutral mixture was filtered through 20 μm filter (Whatman quantitative filter paper, ashless, Grade 41). The filtrate was further filtered by passing it through a 1 kDa ultrafiltration membrane in an ultrafitration system (Spectrum, SYR2-U10-A). Using a rotary evaporator, the ultrafiltered mixture was concentrated (Ika; RV10) to harvest N and S co-doped Carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs/coal-derived CQDs) and determined the yield. The fabricated coal-derived CQDs from both coal samples are dubbed CC-CQDs and JC-CQDs in successive discussions. The colourless and pale-yellow solutions of CQDs were synthesized from Coal-CC and Coal-JC through the process as shown in Fig. S-14.

The reaction parameters such as duration of ultrasonication and volume of H2O2 were systematically varied to optimize the synthesis of coal-derived CQDs and determine the effect on their fluorescence. The fluorescence of these CQDs was determined colorimetrically using quinine sulphate as standard reference in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution at 320 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of raw coal and fabricated coal-derived CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The basic chemistry of the coal samples was analyzed by using a thermogravimetric analyzer (Leco, TGA-701). The carbon, hydrogen and nitrogen content of these samples were determined via CHN analyser (Truspec Leco, 630–100-300), and sulphur content was evaluated using a dual range sulfur analyser (Leco S-144DR). The surface structure of these raw coal samples was examined using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (Make: Zeiss; Model: Sigma). The particle size and structure of the synthesized coal-derived CQDs were determined by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, JEM-2100). For TEM analysis, the specimens were arranged by placing a drop of CQDs suspension on carbon-coated copper grids (300 µm) and letting it air-dry prior to analysis. The TEM images were analysed using Image J (software version1.47). The functional groups in the raw coal samples and coal-derived CQDs were studied using a Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (System-2000, Perkin-Elmer). The nature of the chemical bonding was determined using an X-Ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB Xi + ). The powdered coal samples were first oven dried and kept in desiccator for analysis, while the CQDs (liquid) samples were coated in a small glass slide (12 mm × 6 mm × 1 mm) and dried in a hot air oven (temperature = 50 °C) to form a thick layer before analysis. The crystallinity was studied via X-ray powder diffractometer (Rigaku ULTIMA IV) utilizing Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) at a scanning rate of 2°/min with the start-angle of 5°, stop-angle of 80° for CQDs (for coal start-angle of 3°, stop-angle of 90°) and step-angle 0.05°. The XRD sample preparation technique is similar to that of XPS analysis, but here CQDs samples were coated on a glass slide (75 mm × 25 mm × 1 mm) and kept to dry in a hot air oven (temperature = 50 °C) to form a thick layer before analysis. Using UV–visible spectrophotometer (LABINDIA 1000 + ), the absorption spectra of the coal-derived CQDs was measured. Fluorescence (FL) measurements were performed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, Fluorlolog-3). Time-resolved single-photon counting measurements of the coal-derived CQDs were recorded using the same spectrofluorometer. The surface charges of the synthesized coal-derived CQDs were carried out by using ZETASIZER (Model- Nano ZS, Malvern, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield (QY) of the coal-derived CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (QY) of the coal-derived CQDs was measured via the following equation:
Φ = Φ r × I/Ir × Ar/A × η / η r

Where, “Φ” is the relative QY with respect to the standard, “I” is the measured integrated emission intensity, “η” refers to refractive index of solvent, and “A” refers to optical density (absorbance). Subscript “r” indicates standard index of quinine sulphate. As described in our previous studies, we used a comprehensive approach to determine the quantum yields of the fabricated coal-derived CQDs [27,39].

The rodent hepatocyte cell line, CC1 (SigmaAldrich), was grown in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) medium at 37 °C including 5.5 mM glucose, 10% (v/v) heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% non-essential amino acid (NEAA), 100 U/mL penicillin, 100 μg/mL streptomycin, 12 mM sodium bicarbonate, 20 mM 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) buffer (pH 7), and 2 mM glutamine in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% (v/v) CO2.

Cell viability test was performed using alamarBlueTM reduction bioassay [40]. The alamarBlueTM (Thermo Fisher Scientific), a resazurin-based reagent functions as a cell health indicator. The rodent hepatocyts were seeded at a concentration of 1 million cells/well in 96 wellplates. The cells were systematically processed by increasing concentration of the coal-derived CQDs (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, and 200 µg/mL) in ultrapure water. After treatment for 24 h, the treated media was withdrawn and 0.4% of alamarBlueTM dye was added to the plate. The plate was incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 4 h. The absorbance was measured at 570 nm and 600 nm. These experiments were carried out in triplicates for statistical significance and appropriate controls were included.

The degree of DNA fragmentation or genotoxicity assay were performed by electrophoresis (Thermo scientific, Model: B1A; Powersupply: Bio-rad, Model no: Power Pac HC; voltage used:70 V) of genomic DNA specimens, isolated from rodent hepatocytes, on agarose/ethidium bromide gels by a method as previously stated [40]. The cells were treated with the coal-derived CQDs at a dose of 200 µg/mL for 24hr.

The rodent hepatocytes that were treated with varying concentrations of coal-derived CQDs (5, 10, 20, 50, 100, and 200 µg/mL) for 24 h were tested for their intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS). ROS has been calculated with the fluorescent dye H2DCFDA (2′7′ dichlororofluorescein diacetate). After exposure to coal-derived CQDs, phosphate buffered saline (PBS) was used to wash the cells before treating them by 5 µM H2DCFDA in PBS with 4% Foetal Bovine Serum (FBS). The cells were incubated at 37 °C for 30 min in the dark. After being rinsed in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) comprising 0.5 percent Triton X-100, the cells were collected by centrifugation at 12,000 G (3000 rpm) for 10 min. Intracellular ROS levels were read at excitation and emission wavelengths of 466 and 530 nm, respectively, with fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fluorolog, Horiba Scientific).

Stevia rebaudiana plants were grown for two months in sandy loam soil inside a greenhouse (8 m × 5 m = 40 m2, height = 6 m) at CSIR-CIMAP, Lucknow, India. They were exposed to 16 h of light and the temperature was maintained at, 27/22 ± 2 °C day/night temperature, with an indoor air humidity of 65–80%. Two concentrations (75 mg/L and 150 mg/L) of each coal-derived CQDs were sprayed on the stevia plant (30 mL doses). The plants were grown for three months and fresh leaves were harvested for testing their enzymatic activity. The plants were collected at the start of flowering, rinsed with deionized water, and their weight and length were determined. Plant tissues (root, shoot, and leaves) were allowed to dry at 65 °C for 48 h before being crushed into coarse powder. The terms T1, T2, T3, and T4 are used to indicate the concentration of coal-derived CQDs; T1 and T2 (75 mg/L and 150 mg/L) of JC-CQDs and T3 and T4 (75 mg/L and 150 mg/L) of CC-CQDs.

The concentration of total chlorophyll in fresh leaf tissue was measured using the process described by Arnon and Whatley [41]. For stress enzymes extraction, fresh Stevia rebaudiana leaf tissue was homogenized in 2.0 mL of extraction buffer solution [50 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.5), 0.5 mM ascorbate and 1 mM EDTA] at ice-cold environment. The mixture was then centrifuged for at 10,000 rpm 15 min. The activity of stress-induced antioxidant enzymes as superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT) was examined by the approach outlined by Kakkar et al. [42] and Luck et al. [43], respectively. The protein content was evaluated in crude enzyme extract as per the process mentioned by Lowry et al. [44] Proline content was estimated via the procedure presented by Bates et al. [45].

For the analysis of plant metabolites 100 mg dry and ground leaves of Stevia rebaudiana were taken and added into 1 mL 30% acetonitrile, then sonicated for 30 min. This process was repeated for three times then centrifuged at 10000 rpm (G-force = 1.118 × 105) for 10 mins and supernatant was collected and makeup the final volume up to 5 mL with 30% acetonitrile.

Metabolite in leaves of Stevia rebaudiana were identified using HPLC analysis. The HPLC was performed with column CAPCELL PAK C18 Reverse phase (5 μm, 250x4.6 mm). (a) 10 mmol/L Sodium phosphate buffer (pH-2.6) and (b) acetonitrile (68:32v/v) at flow rate 1.0 mL/min, column temperature at 40 °C, detection at 210 nm, injection volume 5 μL, and total run time 30mins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical properties of the feed coal in its natural state
ce:section.ce:para: The raw coal samples are composed of carbon (72 wt%), hydrogen (5–6 wt%), ash content (2–12 wt%), and total sulphur content (2–3 wt%) (see Table S-1). The percentages of total sulphur present in the feedstock were found to be 3.80% and 2.93% for CC-Coal and JC-Coal, respectively typical of high-sulphur coals [46]. The XRD patterns of CC-Coal show a sharp peak at 26.4° while JC-Coal shows broad peak at 24.7°. The most prominent peak around 26° corresponds to the (002) graphitic lattice spacing as was observed by Duraia et al.[47] and Liu et al. [48] (see Fig. S-1a). The strong diffraction peak observed at around 26° is ascribed to the presence of crystalline carbon in the samples, while sharp spikes corresponds to inorganic components such as pyrite, quartz, and kaolinite. The FTIR spectra analysis of the coal samples (Fig. S-1b) suggests the existence of C = C, C–H, C-S, S = O, and N–H bonds, and CH2/CH3 functional groups. The Raman spectra (see Fig. S-1c) shows the presence of D-bands (∼1380 cm−1) and G-bands (∼1600 cm−1), where D-band indicates the occurrence of defects in the structural units and G-band reflects sp2 carbon networks, both characteristic features of graphitic materials in coal samples. The survey scan XPS spectra of the feed coal samples shows distinct peaks for sulphur (160 eV, 166 eV), carbon (282.8 eV), nitrogen (400 eV), and oxygen (534.5 eV), (Fig. S-2a,b) as also observed in the chemical analysis of the coal samples. In the de-convoluted C1s (Fig. S-2c,d) and O1s (Fig. S-2e,f) signals of feed coal samples, two specific peaks with binding energy around 283.7 eV (sp2 C–C) and 287.7 eV (C = O) were observed for C1s. C = C is the major form of carbon present in the structure of the coal samples, signifying its aromatic structure along with the existence of C = O groups. For O1s, peak was observed at 531.5 eV, which is suggestive of organic C-O linkages. This peak signifies the presence of aromatic clusters linked by connecting bonds through oxygen. The SEM images of the raw coal samples (Fig. S-3a,c) reveals the presence of micron-sized irregular shaped particles reminiscent of amorphous carbon flakes with irregular agglomeration of minerals. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) identified the existence of carbon and oxygen alongside sulphur and trace amounts of iron, silicon, and aluminium (Fig. S-3b,d), all characteristic of high-sulfur coals from Northeastern India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical characterization of synthesized coal-derived CQDs and process optimization

ce:section.ce:para: Table S-2 (see supporting information) summarizes the feed coal sample used and the variation in experimental parameters for the production of CQDs. As seen in Fig. S-4–7 (see supplementary information), the particles were found to be well dispersed in the TEM images when the reaction mixture was ultrasonicated for 6 h. The Quantum Yield (QY) of the coal-derived CQDs under different reaction conditions and amount of solvent used (Table S-2) showed maximum QY nearly equal to 10 %. These coal-derived CQDs are found to be equivalent to those documented by other research groups applied drastic treatments [24,25,29,39] as shown in Table S-3. A maximum QY of 9.95 % is shown by CC6-CDs3 (as shown in Fig. 1 ) signifying it to be the prominent quantum yield amongst all the synthesized CQDs in the current study, indicating its relatively superior optical properties. Ultrasonicating 50 g of raw coal for 6 h in 550-ml H2O2 (i.e. 1:10 solid to liquid ratio) resulting in high-quality fluorescing CQDs with a mean particle diameter of 6 nm. Longer sonication (7 h) led to particles with smaller fluorescence intensity likely due to increased funtionalization of the carbon particles. In contrast, shorter ultrasonication times (4 and 5 h) resulted in particles exhibiting lower fluorescence.

On the basis of the optimization study at various parameters (Table S-2), the process flow sheet (Fig. S-15) has been prepared and it is evident that the process is simple, lesser in step, facile, and pollution-free method, which adopts low-grade coal and environmentally friendly hydrogen peroxide whereas high-grade coal as well as drastic H2SO4 and HNO3 were used by Ye et al. [15]. In this case, the neutralization step is simple and lesser volume of water is required in the neutralization step. Further, the product characteristics are also very much different in comparison to Ye et al. [15]. In view of this, the present process is easy to control, environmentally friendly, has a simple preparation process, and uses low-grade non-steam coals as raw material leading to an economically scalable commercial production.

Although the chemical structures of coals are too complicated to be fully defined, it is crucial to have a thorough understanding of their structural aspects in order to make effective use of coals. The unique chemical arrangement of coal bestows additional benefit over pure sp2-hybridised carbon, such as graphite, due to its disordered conformation and small crystalline carbon areas connected by weak carbon units. Boruah et al. [49] reported the presence of 74% aromatic fraction and 26% aliphatic fraction in coal through X-ray scattering analysis of polycyclic aromatic unit of subbituminous coal. The organic component of coal mainly constitutes of polycyclic aromatic, hydroaromatic, and heterocyclic clusters combined by aliphatic and ether bonds to form a cross-linked three-dimensional network. Alkyl side chains along with phenolic hydroxyl and ether groups substitute into aromatic rings. These complex cross-linked aromatic ring structures possesses varying amounts of inorganic and organically bound sulfur (<1 to 6%), nitrogen (<1 to 2%) and oxygen. In subbituminous coals, the main oxygen functional groups are phenolic or etheric, with higher amounts of carboxylic acids or esters along with carbonyls [50]. Nitrogen and sulfur are mainly located in heterocyclic compounds. It was reported that low-rank coals possess notable amounts of aliphatic sulphur (dialkyl sulfides) along with aromatic sulfur (thiophenes and diaryl sulfides) [51,52]. In low-rank coals, it was reported that pyrrolic and quaternary nitrogen was most abundantly available with lower amounts of pyridinic or basic nitrogen forms [53,54]. Besides, oxygen-containing functional group such as phenolic hydroxyl group (Ar–OH), carboxyl (−COOH), methoxyl (−O–CH3), and ether oxygen atom (R–O − R) are abundantly present in the complex structures of coal.

The self co-doped NS-CQDs (coal-derived CQDs) synthesized using a similar method was reported to have polycyclic aromatic and hydroaromatic frameworks [39]. Even though several studies have documented the plausible graphical representation for synthesis of CQDs [23,26], mechanisms of doping remain unclear. A probable mechanism has been illustrated in Fig. 2 which is hypothesized to be similar to the mechanism of CQDs demonstrated elsewhere [23,26,55]. The crystalline fragments within the microstructure of coal are bound via organic carbon linkages. The OH . radicals produced during the H2O2 treatment is a strong non-specific oxidant that breaks the organic carbon bonds into CO2 and H2O, producing stable and crystalline nanosized carbon (CQDs). This approach oxidized the coal samples in H2O2, allowing the oxidant to penetrate which in turn oxidised the interlayers of the graphitic structure of coals. The interlayer gap and defoliation degree increased as the oxidizer entered further leading to breakage of the bridge bonds like -O-, -S-, –CH2-O- and –CH2-CH2-, etc., resulting in CQDs. Ultrasonication-induced cavitation cleaves the bonds connecting the graphite with polycyclic aromatic fragments in coal. The aromatic fragments potentially split into C2 units, eventually resulting in polymorphic reactions yielding CQDs. As reported by Wang and Yang [56], the synthesis of carbon nanostructures is assumed to be based on the metastable phase generation at the nanoscale. The rupture of the weak covalent bonds, breaking of hydrogen bonds and the loosening of п-п interactions were assumed to be responsible for reactions that leads to the formation of smaller nanostructures.

The current ultrasonic treatment impacted the coal particles, causing cavitation and evacuation of sulfur compounds found in raw coal samples, resulting in cracking [57]. As mentioned above, the hydrogen and hydroxyl radicals generated from H2O2 were responsible for the oxidation of coal [58]. The sulphur components on oxidation get converted to sulfoxide, sulfone, and sulphates, respectively, leading to the breakage of the bonds within coal samples. Furthermore, these findings were attributed to ultrasonic waves that results in the penetration of ultrasonic energy through the coal slurry. This resulted in bubble cavitation, which is responsible for the breakdown of bonds within coal samples. These breakdown of the coal structure releases part of the organically bonded sulfur, oxygen, and nitrogen components as volatiles. As a result, we can say that the presence of high sulphur in the low-grade coal plays a vital role in the formation of CQDs.

The TEM/HR-TEM images of both the coal-derived CQDs show uniform particle distribution with spherical shape as shown in Fig. 3 a,b,e,f. The particle size distribution of CC-CQDs (Fig. 3c) ranged between 4 and 8 nm in diameter, having mean diameter of 6 nm, while that of JC-CQDs (Fig. 3g) ranged within 3.8 and 6 nm, having mean diameter of 5 nm. The crystallinity and phase of the fabricated coal-derived CQDs were established using HR-TEM in association with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern. The SAED patterns of CC-CQDs displays d-spacing of 0.204 and 0.359 nm in Fig. 3a (inset) which signifies the (101) and (002) planes of graphitic carbon. Likewise, the JC-CQDs shows the lattice spacing of 0.277, 0.203 and 0.162 nm in Fig. 3e (inset) indicating the (100), (101), and (004) planes of graphitized carbon. The SAED patterns of CC-CQDs depict diffused rings with bright spots indicates to be of single crystalline nature, while the diffused rings of JC-CQDs signifies the amorphous/ partially crystalline nature of carbon structures. The findings obtained were similar to those described in the previous studies [59,60]. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) (Fig. 3d and h), confirms the existence of C, O, N, and S. The elemental mapping (Fig. S-10) and EDS study of CC-CQDs and JC-CQDs confirms the presence of C, O, N, and S from which it can also be referred to as self co-doped NS-CQDs derived from coal in-build with such elements. Additionally, the chemical structural properties of the as-synthesised self co-doped NS-CQDs revealed to be identical to those explained for CQDs formation [12,26,61,62].

XRD patterns of synthesized coal-derived CQDs (Fig. 4 a) depicts an intense wide peak centred at around 2θ = 24.12° for CC-CQDs and 2θ = 23.14° for JC-CQDs having interlayer spacing of 0.369 and 0.3844 nm that corresponds to the (002) graphitic crystal plane. The observed peak indicates the conjugate sp2 carbon framework that signifies the aromatic structures. The reported peak is found to be close to the graphitic peak 2θ = 26.4°. The deviation from initial graphitic peak could be attributed to the presence of oxygen possessing functional groups corresponding to the amorphous carbon phases [55,63]. This validates the poor crystalline nature of these coal-derived CQDs that is consistent with the SAED patterns depicted in Fig. 3a and e (inset).

As illustrated in Fig. 4b, FTIR analysis of the coal-derived CQDs exhibit absorption peaks due to the presence of various functional groups for instance O–H, N–H, C = C, C = O, C–H, C-O, C-N, and C-S. The broad absorption bands observed at 3400, 3190, and 3020 cm−1 are due to stretching mode of O–H, N–H, and C–H, respectively. The bands observed at 2865 and 2990 cm−1 are due to C–H stretching vibration and is more prominent in JC-CQDs. Moreover, an absorption band around 1680 cm−1 is due to stretching mode of C = C bond. In JC-CQDs, a prominent absorption band at 1580 cm−1 is observed due to the stretching vibrations of C = O. In addition, absorption bands at 1300 and 1410 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching modes of S-O and bending vibration of C-N in synthesized CQDs. The absorption band at around 1401 cm−1 could be attributed to ammonium (NH4 +) vibration [64]. The presence of a sharp bands at 1075 cm−1 is assigned to the presence of the stretching modes of S = O or C-O bond. Another absorption peak at 619 cm−1 corresponds to stretching modes of the C-S bond. These results obtained from FTIR analysis were found to be similar to those described for doped CQDs [62,64,65].

The survey scan XPS spectra in Fig. S-11 a and b confirms that the coal-derived CQDs are comprised of C, O, N, and S and show strong signals of the C1s and O1s peaks at a binding energies of 283 and 532 eV in addition to weak signals for N1s and S2p near 400 and 168 eV, respectively. The deconvoluted XPS spectra of C1s for CC-CQDs (Fig. 5 a) depicts significant peaks at 283.5, 285 and 287.2 eV correlating to sp2 C–C, C-OH, and O-C = O, respectively. The C1s spectrum for JC-CQDs (Fig. 5e) shows major peaks at 283.4, 287.2, and 289.8 eV correlating to sp2 C–C, O-C = O, and C = O, respectively [39,66,67]. The C1s spectrum of both the coal-derived CQDs shows a signal at 286.7 eV that could be attributed to the C-O/C-S/C-N bonds. The O1s deconvoluted spectrum for the coal-derived CQDs (Fig. 5b and f) suggests that there is organic C-O bond at 531 eV, but an extra peak at 533 eV (Fig. 5f) is noticed due to organic C = O bond. The deconvoluted spectra of N1s, (Fig. 5c and g) shows binding energies at 389.8 and 400.5 eV that are ascribed to the presence of C-N and pyrrolic nitrogen. In addition, high resolution deconvoluted spectra of S2p as depicted in Fig. 5d and h shows two peaks at 167.6 and 168.8 eV, indicating S = O and C-S groups. The spectra also signifies that the maximum intensity of S = O binding energy in JC-CQDs is higher than that of CC-CQDs. Thus, the effective self-doping of N and S elements on the fabricated coal-derived CQDs matrix was examined by the results obtained from FTIR and XPS characterizations. Table S-3 reveals the atomic percentages (%) of C, O, N, and S in NS-CQDs. These doped elements were exclusively extracted from the initial elements present in subbituminous coal which had formerly been confirmed via elemental, spectroscopic, and electron beam analysis (Table S-1, Fig. S-2b, S-2, S-4). The results obtained from FTIR and XPS analysis, revealed the surface being chemically modified by oxygen-rich groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl along with sp2 hybridised aromatic fragments [12,68]. Owing to the existence of the oxygen-rich functional groups, coal-derived CQDs have been assumed to be highly hydrophilic in nature making them beneficial for advanced studies on their toxicological assessment.

The Raman spectroscopy of N,S-CQDs (for CC-CQDs) shown in Fig. S-12 demonstrates two distinct bands at 1440 cm−1 for D-band and 1611 cm−1 for G-band respectively. The peak in the range of 1440 cm−1 refers to be D-band signifying the sp3hybridized carbon frameworks [69]. The peak at about 1614 cm−1 is related to the G-band, corresponding to the sp2 carbon frameworks [70]. The sp2 hybridized carbon frameworks correlated to G- and D-bands formed due to lattice defects possessing the sp3-hybridized carbon. Based on the intensity of both D and G-band, the fabricated N,S-CQDs can be referred to be both aliphatic and aromatic (graphitic) in nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observations from UV–vis and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopic analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The photo-physical properties of the coal-derived CQDs were analyzed by means of UV–vis and FL spectroscopy, as displayed in Fig. 6 . Under natural light (Fig. 6a) CC-CQDs and JC-CQDs suspensions are colourless and pale yellow, while on excitation under UV source of 365-nm wavelength, synthesized coal-derived CQDs display bright blue and cyan emission, respectively. Fig. 6(b) depicts the absorbance spectrum of the diluted solution of the resultant coal-derived CQDs. The absorption band observed between 250 and 270 nm may possibly be ascribed to π-π* transitions of sp2 carbon core of the aromatic system. In addition, CQDs shows strong absorption in 250–300 nm region that is preferably referred to n-π* (nO 2p–π* and nN 2p–π*) transitions within energy levels from surface domains originating from the carboxyl/carbonyl/amine/ammonium carboxylate functional groups [39,64,71]. The UV absorption spectrum signifies the coal-derived CQDs to be of varied size along with different surface functional groups which is compatible with the findings of TEM and FTIR/XPS analysis.

The FL spectra (Fig. 6c and d) of the coal-derived CQDs display an excitation dependent behaviour that has been discussed in prior studies [11,31,62]. The highest intensity of FL spectra for CC-CQDs was observed to be between 430 and 440 nm, representing the blue region of the spectrum, while JC-CQDs shows maximum emission at 450 nm, signifying the cyan colour. Besides, with increasing excitation wavelength, highest emission of FL spectra is slightly red-shifted with decreasing FL intensity. At 320-nm excitation of JC-CQDs the FL intensity slightly increases compared to 300-nm excitation, which further decreases with gradual increase in the excitation wavelength [72]. This confirms that the synthesized coal-derived-CQDs possess size-varied nanoparticles (quantum effect) and diverse surface emissive traps on these nanoparticles [72–74]. In this view, the important objective would be to differentiate the consequence of quantum confinement effects that may be correlated with the presence of aromatic π-domains of the carbon cores, surface states that results from the presence of carbon system-related functional groups, and molecular states that gives rise to emission resulting from fluorescent-free or bound structures. The FL properties of CQDs can also be evaluated by varied particle sizes or functional groups on carbon nano-particle surface [75,76]. FTIR and XPS analysis suggest the availability of fluorophore or chromophore groups in aromatic structures. Assuming the FL properties, the nN 2p–σ* transition connected with amino groups, π–π* transition associated with the sp2 domain of the aromatic system, and transition from nO 2P–π* of the protonated carboxyl excitation wavelength, the maximum emission of the FL spectra groups is ascribed to show blue fluorescence [77,78]. Besides, the transition from nO 2P–π* of de-protonated carboxyl groups may assume to show cyan fluorescence [64]. The presence of several fluorescent cores in coal-derived CQDs has so boosted the prospect of FL emission that is excitation-dependent, and it is also possible to demonstrate the varied emissions from different fluorescent cores. In addition, Sciortino et al. [79] reported that the blue fluorescence may also result from minute organic fluorophores that are attached to carbon nanostructure surfaces but not directly to the dots.

The average FL lifetimes of the fabricated coal-derived CQDs were determined using time-resolved single-photon counting spectroscopy. Fig. 6e displays the time-resolved FL decay curves and the average FL lifetimes of CC-CQDs and JC-CQDs at neutral pH were noticed to be 80.078 and 80.476 ns respectively. As both the coal-derived CQDs nearly possess identical carbon core, the difference in FL lifetime revealed the impact of functional groups on the surface of carbon nanostructures FL activity. These reported lifetimes suggest that the fabricated coal-derived CQDs could be used in biological applications.

The estimated FL emission QY of the coal-derived CQDs, namely CC-CQDs and JC-CQDs, seemed to be 9.35 % and 16.96 %, respectively, with quinine sulphate as reference with excitation at 320 nm. From this analysis, we can say that the CQDs derived from low grade coal shows higher QY than that of higher-grade coal. As it was reported by Ye et al. [23], the QY of high grade coal, i.e., anthracite and bituminous coal were found to be 1 %. In another work reported by our research group, the QY of high grade coal were found to be 0.53, 0.20 and 0.04 % respectively. So, it can be mentioned that heteroatoms-doped CQDs, likely nitrogen (N) and sulphur (S), can be recognized as one of the most realistic strategies to improve QYs by adding more coordination sites and introducing more defects. The percent of heteroatoms doped in low-grade coal is comparatively higher than that of high-grade coal which may be a reason for the higher QYs in the low grade coals. These coal-derived CQDs are found to be similar/higher than those reported by other research works [24,25,29,39], as shown in Table S-4. The concentrations of the CQDs were estimated to be 7.11 mg/mL and 8.11 mg/mL, respectively. The production yield of the CC-CQDs and JC-CQDs were approximately found to about 1620 mL and 1635 mL, respectively. These findings validate the effective fabrication of highly hydrophilic and strong blue and cyan fluorescence of CQDs.

The zeta potential was also investigated to determine the stability and surface charge of the coal-derived CQDs. As represented in Fig. 6f CC-CQDs and JC-CQDs has a zeta potential value of −12.1 and −9.91 mV, indicating the surface of coal-derived CQDs to be of negative polarity. The negative zeta values signify the fabricated coal-derived CQDs to be highly stable. Due to abundant oxygen-possessing functional groups (such as carboxyl/carbonyl/amine) associated in the coal-derived CQDs surface, resulted in negative values. The hydrophilic groups considerably enhance its solubility and sustainability in aqueous media, making it utilization in biochemical and biomedical activities much easier.

One of the most significant components of drug innovation research is cell-based assessment. The cytotoxicity consequences of the fabricated coal derived CQDs on cell viability and cell death were investigated. The rodent hepatocyte cell lines treated with varying the doses of CQDs (5, 10, 20, 50, 100, and 200 µg/mL for 24 h) revealed cell viability above 90% (Fig. 7 a). This indicates that the CQDs did not cause cytotoxic effects when correlated to control for all the concentrations that were tested. Both CC-CQDs and JC-CQDs show almost similar results with a highest cell viability of 93.62 and 93.06 %, respectively, for 10 µg/mL concentration. Lowest cell viability of 89.57 and 91.37 % is shown by CC-CQDs and JC-CQDs for 200 µg/mL concentration. It is observed that even at higher concentration, its cytotoxicity did not increase much, which may be considered to show good cell viability. It's worth noting that the presence of cell friendly NH2 groups in the synthesized coal-derived CQDs made them compatible for pharmaceutical applications [80]. The lack of cytotoxicity could be potentially ascribed to the use of non-hazardous precursors and effective removal of unwanted elemental impurities. This analysis reveals that coal-derived CQDs have comparable biocompatibility and are non-toxic on hepatocyte cell lines, suggesting potential applications for bioimaging. These findings are consistent with the biocompatibile nature of CQDs derived from other sources [28,66,80,81].

To examine the genotoxicity of coal derived CQDs, the pattern of DNA fragmentation in hepatocyte cell lines were studied. When cells were treated with both the coal-derived CQDs samples at a concentration of 200 µg/mL for 24 h, there was no evidence of DNA damage in the treated cells when correlated to the standard cells (Fig. 8 ).

The reactive oxygen species (ROS) analyzed using varying concentrations of coal-derived CQDs demonstrated no significant change in the generation of intracellular ROS levels compared to the control group (Fig. 7b). This suggests that these coal-derived CQDs did not elevate the production of ROS that plays an important role in the oxidative stress pathway. Zhou et al. [82], for instance, has published similar findings. This toxicological characterization further suggests the suitability of coal-derived CQDs for biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of coal-derived CQDs on plant metabolites
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of coal-derived CQDs on plant metabolites with a view on the effect of plant growth has been investigated. Fig. 9 shows the photographs of Stevia rebaudiana plants under study for different treatments to examine the impact of coal-derived CQDs on plant metabolites. The amendment of coal-derived CQDs was shown to improve the production of plant biomass correlated to the control (Fig. 10 a). The growth of plant (Fig. 10b) and significant increase in the length of the leaves (Fig. 10c) compared to the control is presumably observed due to the presence of various hydrophilic groups involving hydroxyl and carboxyl and amino functional groups on the coal-derived CQDs surface that offers additional binding sites for water molecules to penetrate the plant. These functional groups bind nutrients by hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions for controlled release. As a result, sufficient moisture in plant can encourage expedited plant growth. Chandrakar et al. [83] demonstrated that CDs can promote (till 66%) plant growth even in the availability of arsenic. As observed from Fig. 10d and e, there has been an increase in chlorophyll and protein content of stevia plants treated with coal-derived CQDs. The treated plants had higher Stevioside content (Fig. 10f) as compared to Rebaudioside A (Fig. 10g). Both metabolites were significantly higher in CC-CQDs treatments. The ratio of Rebaudioside A/Stevioside was 0.048 in the control. However, CC-CQDs had a similar Rebaudioside A/Stevioside ratio as in the control which indicates that there is no positive or negative effect of these coal-derived CQDs on the metabolite contents of this stevia plant. The impact of defense-related gene expression in Cicer arietinum L. by CDs has been reported by Chandrakar et al. [83] Application of CQDs formulations was also reported elsewhere for the production of secondary siRNA, and the systemic spread of silencing [84]. The activity of major antioxidants superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT) and proline (Fig. 10 h, i and j) showed higher antioxidant property compared to the control. Under stress conditions, protein structures are broken down into amino acids and transformed to proline, which is then stored. The higher SOD, proline, and catalase activities are observed in higher concentration (150 mg/L) of JC-CQDs, which showed that coal-derived CQDs induce stress to plants. Application of CQDs formulations on defense-related gene expression in Cicer arietinum L. was reported previously [83,84]. It might be possible that CQDs in present study enhanced the defence related gene expression causing release of stress enzymes. The multidimensional scaling (MDS) analysis demonstrated that the T1 (JC-CQDs) and T4 (CC-CQDs) treatments showing dissimilarity between control and other treatments (see Fig. S-13). Treatment, T1 successfully shows greater plant length as well as higher chlorophyll content but in the case of metabolite (rebaudioside A), it was observed to be lower than the control. In the same way, treatment T4 successfully showing higher biomass yield as well as a metabolite (rebaudioside A) and providing low stress. Moreover, CC-CQDs could be used as a plant growth promoter.

The steady and unique fluorescence signal of CQDs offers a feasible method for revealing CQD uptake by plants. Furthermore, because of their fluorescence, CQDs can be used as a UV-to-blue light alteration medium in plants to accelerate its rate of photosynthesis and to detect them in plants [85]. These CQDs can also be used for bio-imaging of plant cells. In view of the above results, these coal-derived CQDs can be used as a carrier of nutrients and microbes for plant growth promoter and also has potential to act as nanofertilizer.

We have devised a simplistic, green, and cost-efficient route for fabricating two fluorescence-emitting nitrogen and sulfur self/co-doped CQDs (coal-derived CQDs) via an ultrasonic wet-chemical process at room-temperature using inexpensive sub-bituminous coal as a precursor. The optimization of the synthesis parameters of the proposed process for the fabrication of coal-derived CQDs is very important. To the best of our knoledge, it is the very first evidence of large/gram-scale fabrication of CQDs using inexpensive coal. The synthesized coal-derived CQDs possess very strong fluorescing properties with high solubility in water, which resulted from abundant oxygen possessing groups on the surface of CQDs. Maximum fluorescence emission QY of the fabricated NS-CQDs (coal-derived CQDs) is identified to be 16.96 %. These CQDs display good cell viability (about 90%) and did not elevate the formation of reactive oxygen species at concentration within 5–200 µg/mL. In addition, these coal-derived CQDs have so far proved to be non-genotoxic and bio-compatible indicating its potential in bio-imaging and in vivo study. Moreover, these coal-derived CQDs also have proved to be a good carrier of nutrients and microbes for plant growth promotion, effectively behaving as a nanofertilizer. The outcomes emphasize the significance of large-scale fabrication of self co-doped NS-CQDs (i.e., coal-derived CQDs) and its future aspects on clinical diagnostic applications.
